risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Eligible women were randomized to one of the two arms according to a pre-defined randomization list prepared at ITM, Antwerp. Participants were assigned sequential study numbers which were matched with numbered envelopes containing the arm allocation that were opened by the study nurses only after recruitment of the study subject." Comment: This describes a pre-defined randomization list with numbered envelopes and sequential study numbers, and the study was stratified by HIV status. These context clues indicate a proper random allocation process, so random sequence generation is Low risk.

_evidence.method_quote: Eligible women were randomized to one of the two arms according to a pre-defined randomization list prepared at ITM, Antwerp. Participants were assigned sequential study numbers which were matched with numbered envelopes containing the arm allocation that were opened by the study nurses only after recruitment of the study subject.
_evidence.method_summary: Randomization used a pre-defined randomization list with sequential study numbers matched to numbered envelopes opened after recruitment.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Participants were assigned sequential study numbers which were matched with numbered envelopes containing the arm allocation that were opened by the study nurses only after recruitment of the study subject." Comment: This describes a concealment process using sequentially numbered envelopes opened only after enrollment, which prevents foreknowledge of the next assignment. Although the article does not explicitly say the envelopes were opaque and sealed, the stated mechanism is an explicit allocation-concealment procedure that protects against recruiter prediction.

_evidence.concealment_quote: Participants were assigned sequential study numbers which were matched with numbered envelopes containing the arm allocation that were opened by the study nurses only after recruitment of the study subject.
_evidence.note: Sequence generation is described (“pre-defined randomization list prepared at ITM, Antwerp”), but the article does not explicitly state that the envelopes were opaque and sealed.

risk_of_bias.domain: Blinding of participants and personnel (performance bias)
risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a phase 3 randomized open label clinical trial... There was no blinding as each of the study drugs was openly administered." Comment: The study explicitly states that participants and personnel were not blinded (open-label, openly administered drugs). Because the trial also assessed outcomes that could plausibly be influenced by knowledge of treatment allocation (e.g. adverse events and some pregnancy outcomes/clinical assessments), this creates a dominant risk of performance bias across the study overall.

_evidence.blinding_quote: This was a phase 3 randomized open label clinical trial... There was no blinding as each of the study drugs was openly administered.
_evidence.blinding_method: none; open label, each of the study drugs was openly administered

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "This was a phase 3 randomized open label clinical trial... There was no blinding as each of the study drugs was openly administered." Comment: The study explicitly states there was no blinding, and outcome assessment included subjective/judgement-dependent outcomes such as adverse events, with causality assessed by attending physicians. Because participants and likely assessors were unblinded and at least one outcome was subjective, detection bias is high for the domain under the worst-outcome rule.

_evidence.assessor_blinding_quote: This was a phase 3 randomized open label clinical trial whose planned primary endpoint was placental malaria. ... There was no blinding as each of the study drugs was openly administered.

risk_of_bias.support_text: Quote: "Due to loss to follow up and consent withdrawal (Figure 2), 166 pregnant women in the CTX arm and 145 in SP arm were analyzed at delivery." Comment: The analysed denominators are reported and match the randomized counts for the main analytic comparison at delivery (CTX 166/166; SP 145/145 in the extracted flow), indicating essentially complete outcome data for the reported delivery outcomes, with no meaningful post-randomization loss apparent on the analysed outcome. Although the paper also states that analyses used complete cases, the reported per-arm analysed numbers at delivery do not suggest attrition that would bias this domain.

per_arm_flow.reasons: “Due to loss to follow up and consent withdrawal (Figure 2), 166 pregnant women in the CTX arm ... were analyzed at delivery.”
“Due to loss to follow up and consent withdrawal (Figure 2), ... 145 in SP arm were analyzed at delivery.”

_evidence.notes: The article reports per-arm randomized and analyzed numbers at delivery, but does not give arm-specific losses or a CONSORT-style flow with exact post-randomization attrition counts in the text provided. The analysis is described as ITT but also as complete-case.
